region_of_origin: China; widely adopted in Singapore, Malaysia, and Hong Kong

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for light soy sauce with cut chillies. Closest match is the generic soy sauce food product class. The chilli component is not captured in this ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers soy sauce generically. No specific code exists for the chilli-infused variant. Used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific condiment combination. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (wheat typically present in soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 15 ml (one tablespoon), providing about 10 kcal and 877 mg of sodium, used as a dipping sauce or condiment alongside a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Soy sauce made from soy and wheat (shoyu) and Peppers, hot chili, red, raw; values combined proportionally for light soy sauce with fresh chilli condiment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for light soy sauce with cut chillies as a combined condiment. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for soy sauce (shoyu, FDC ID 16124) and raw red chilli peppers (FDC ID 11819), combined in an approximate 85:15 ratio by weight reflecting typical preparation. Sodium value cross-referenced against Singapore Health Promotion Board data on sodium content of common food seasonings (HPB, 2024). Micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, potassium) are elevated relative to plain soy sauce due to the chilli component and are consistent with USDA chilli pepper data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soy sauce and condiment analogues — estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for fermented soy-based condiments and low-carbohydrate sauces
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test exists for light soy sauce with cut chillies. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for soy sauce and fermented condiments, which consistently show GI values in the 10–20 range due to negligible starch content and high sodium/protein matrix. Glycemic load per 15 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 × 1.425) / 100 ≈ 0.2. The chilli component contributes negligible additional carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content — 5850 mg per 100 g; a single 15 ml serving delivers approximately 877 mg sodium, representing approximately 38% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid this condiment; even a single tablespoon provides a clinically significant sodium load, and low-sodium soy sauce alternatives should be substituted where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and sugar content per serving; GI is estimated at 15 (low); capsaicin from chillies may modestly support insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This condiment is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used in small quantities, as the glycemic load per serving is negligible (approximately 0.2), though the high sodium content warrants caution in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content poses a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; however, fat and cholesterol content are negligible.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should use this condiment sparingly or replace it with a reduced-sodium variant, as the high sodium load can exacerbate hypertension and increase cardiovascular event risk despite the absence of saturated fat or cholesterol.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Capsaicin and vitamin C from chillies have potential anti-inflammatory properties, but high sodium intake may be pro-inflammatory in some individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Moderate use may be acceptable; the anti-inflammatory potential of capsaicin is modest, and the high sodium content should be considered in the context of overall dietary sodium management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Light Soy Sauce with Cut Chillies (辣椒豉油, Là jiāo chǐ yóu) is one of the most fundamental and ubiquitous condiments in Chinese and Southeast Asian Chinese cuisine, found on virtually every table in Singapore, Malaysia, and Hong Kong. It is prepared by combining premium light soy sauce — a thin, pale-amber, intensely salty fermented soy product — with freshly sliced bird's eye chillies (cili padi), which may be red or green. The preparation requires no cooking; the chillies are simply cut and submerged in the soy sauce, releasing their capsaicin, volatile oils, and vitamin C into the liquid. The result is a sharp, salty, umami-rich dipping sauce with a clean, penetrating heat that varies in intensity depending on the ratio of chillies used. Texturally, it is a thin liquid with visible chilli rings floating throughout. It is served as an accompaniment to steamed fish, Hainanese chicken rice, dim sum, fried noodles, congee, and almost any savory Chinese dish. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (65 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (0.2 g), and provides moderate protein (6.5 g) from the fermented soy base. Its most clinically significant characteristic is its extremely high sodium content — approximately 5850 mg per 100 g — making it a major dietary sodium source even when consumed in small quantities. A single 15 ml tablespoon delivers approximately 877 mg of sodium. The chilli component contributes vitamin C (15.5 mg per 100 g), vitamin A (45 mcg RAE), and potassium (280 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible at approximately 0.2. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans. Clinically, it is contraindicated or should be strictly limited in individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or chronic kidney disease due to its sodium load.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for soy sauce (shoyu) and raw red chilli peppers combined in an approximate 85:15 ratio, cross-referenced with Singapore HPB sodium data. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact combined condiment. The sodium value is well-supported; macronutrient values are plausible and consistent with component data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for fermented soy condiments. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (soy sauce shoyu FDC ID 16124 and raw red chilli FDC ID 11819) in an approximate 85:15 ratio, with sodium cross-referenced against Singapore HPB (2024). No exact combined-product entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — fermented plant-based soy sauce and fresh chillies contain no vitamin B12; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for both components. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — neither soy sauce nor fresh chillies are a source of vitamin D per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats are present in this condiment. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — this is a plant-based condiment with no animal-derived fat. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture data for this specific combined condiment preparation is not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; individual component moisture values differ significantly and a reliable combined figure cannot be defensibly derived. 4. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical 15 g default servings both marked is_default: true) has been resolved to a single canonical 15 g serving. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI test for this condiment exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Ontology anchors use soy sauce as the closest defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 match; the chilli component is not captured in available ontology codes. 7. This condiment is not recommended for individuals on low-sodium diets, those with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or chronic kidney disease. 8.
